revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Bureau of Shinto Affairs]][[ja:神道事務局]][[simple:Bureau of Shinto Affairs]]


{{Short description|Shinto Organization|qq=}}
{{Infobox organization
| name = Bureau of Shinto Affairs
| formation = {{ill|1875|en|1875|qid=Q7764|lt=1875|1=1875}}
| predecessor = [[Taikyo Institute|Great Teaching Institute]]
| successor = [[:en:Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]], [[:en:Association of Sectarian Shinto|Association of Sectarian Shinto]], [[:en:Office of Japanese Classics Research|Office of Japanese Classics Research]]
| dissolved = {{ill|1886|en|1886|qid=Q7822|lt=1886|1=1886}}
}}
{{Nihongo|'''Bureau of Shinto Affairs'''|神道事務局|Shinto Jimukyoku|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=Shinto of Japan |url=https://doyouknowjapan.com/shinto/ |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=Encyclopedia of Japan |language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230315072827/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8853</ref> was the successor to the [[:en:Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]], which was founded in 1875.<ref name="susumu-2009a">{{Cite journal |last1=Susumu |first1=Shimazono |last2=島茴進 |last3=Murphy |first3=Regan E. |date=2009 |title=State Shinto in the Lives of the People: The Establishment of Emperor Worship, Modern Nationalism, and Shrine Shinto in Late Meiji |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30233855 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=36 |issue=1 |pages=93–124 |jstor=30233855 |issn=0304-1042|qq=}}</ref> In the religious administration of the Meiji era, it is an organization that brings together [[Shinto]] factions nationwide. It was a public central institution. [[Meiji Restoration|Meiji Government]] set up a {{ill|Dormitory|en|Dormitory|qid=Q847950|lt=Student Dormitory|1=Dormitory}} at the Bureau of Shinto Affairs to train priests. It was also an accreditation body of [[Sect Shinto]].
It served a purpose of training [[Kyodo Shoku|kyodo shoku]] and over time ran into issues over pantheon disputes.<ref name="susumu-2009a" /> This eventually led to the ascension of the [[:en:Jingukyo|Ise sect]] and the marginalization of the [[Izumo-taishakyo|Izumo sect]].<ref name="susumu-2009a" />
In 1882 it was made into a shinto sect itself due to an ordinance demanding the separation of shrine priests and missionaries or theologians, and in 1884 such missionaries of both shinto and Buddhism were suppressed.<ref name="susumu-2009a" /> The [[:en:Office of Japanese Classics Research|Office of Japanese Classics Research]] was created as a replacement for it{{Citation needed|date=March 2023|qq=}}.
In 1886, it reorganized into the {{Nihongo|Shinto Headquarters|神道本局|Shinto Honkyoku|qq=}} and the name was later changed to '''[[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]'''.<ref>{{Cite web |title=教派神道とは – 神道大教 |url=https://shintotaikyo.org/%e7%a5%9e%e9%81%93%e5%a4%a7%e6%95%99%e3%81%ae%e7%b4%b9%e4%bb%8b/%e7%a5%9e%e9%81%93%e5%a4%a7%e6%95%99%e3%81%a8%e3%81%af/ |access-date=2022-06-26 |language=ja|qq=}}</ref>
In 1912, the so-called ''[[Sect Shinto|The Thirteen Schools of Shinto]]'' came together to form the {{Nihongo3|Association of Sectarian Shinto|教派神道連合会|Kyoha Shintō Rengōkai|qq=}}.{{Citation needed|date=March 2023|qq=}}
== See also ==
* [[:en:Daikyoin|Great Teaching Institute]]
* [[:en:Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]
* [[Kyodo Shoku]]
* [[Sect Shinto]]
